meta.title: Hong Kong Criminal Law Hierarchical Graph
notes: Hybrid hierarchical graph bundle generated from the Casemap relationship graph.
Hierarchy is represented explicitly as Module -> Subground -> Topic while authorities remain graph-native.
Neo4j constraints and import templates are included for database-backed deployment.
meta.viewer_heading_public: Hong Kong Criminal Law Hierarchical Knowledge Graph
meta.viewer_heading_internal: Hong Kong Criminal Law Internal Hierarchy Explorer
meta.viewer_intro_public: A criminal-law hierarchy and authority network built from HKLII judgments plus local textbook passages. Start at the doctrinal modules, then drill into topics, cases, and statutes.
meta.viewer_intro_internal: This internal criminal-law explorer uses the same hierarchy shell but keeps the graph centered on case metadata, textbook support passages, and statute links.

nodes.label: Subject to subsection (6), where an accused and the husband or wife of the accused are standing trial together, neither spouse shall at the trial be competent to give evidence for the prosecution under subsection (1), or be compellable to give evidence under subsection (2) or (3). (7) Section 7 of the Evidence Ordinance (Cap. 8)

nodes.label: Possession of Offensive Weapon in Public Place (1) Any person who, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, has with him in any public place any offensive weapon shall be guilty of an offence and shall be sentenced, on summary conviction or conviction on indictment, in the manner specified in subsection (2). nodes.summary_en: Trafficking in a dangerous drug is a serious offence, which attracts the maximum sentence of life imprisonment: s 4(3)(a) of the Ordinance. The Court of Appeal sets out various sentencing tariffs in respect of different kinds of dangerous drugs, as well as guidelines as to how to approach sentencing drug-trafficking offenders. The maximum sentence for simple possession of dangerous drug is seven years imprisonment: s 8(2)(a) of the Ordinance. The court...

nodes.label: DPP v Doot
nodes.summary_en: The offence of conspiracy cannot exist without t he agreement, consent or combination of two or more persons. See also: HKSAR v Lee Wai Ki & Ors [2001] 2 HKLRD 610, [2001] HKCU 582 (CFI). A conspiracy does not end with the making of the agreement, it continues so long as there are two or more parties to it intending to carry out the design: see DPP v Doot [1973] AC 807 (HL). 1047 Dangerous Drugs Ordinance (Cap 134) [39.01] bwpageid:: 1047:: bwservice::::

nodes.summary_en: Rehearing distinguished from hearing de novo An appeal by rehearing is distinguished from a hearing de novo. A hearing de novo is a retrial. The first trial magistrate’s Statement of Findings is irrelevant. Live evidence is called and fresh findings of facts are made as in any trial. nodes.label: R v Hereford Magistrates
nodes.summary_en: Criminal Law in Hong Kong 30 [1-102] In exercising appellate jurisdiction, a Court of First Instance Judge would hear a magistracy appeal sitting alone. Unlike appeals to the Court of Appeal or the Court of Final Appeal, a magistracy appeal does not require leave. You decide intent by considering what the defendant did or did not do [and the effect of his actions or inaction] and by what he said or did not say.
